HomeAboutProjectsMathsSocials

Introduction

  • Basic encryption process:

    • A has a message (plaintext) which is encrypted using an encryption key to produce the ciphertext, which is sent to BBB.

    • B uses a decryption key (which depends on the encryption key) to decrypt the ciphertext and recover the original plaintext.

    • It should be computationally infeasible to determine the plaintext without knowing the decryption key.

  • Caesar cipher:

    • Add a constant to each letter in the plaintext to produce the ciphertext:  ciphertext letter = plaintext letter +kmod⁡26\text{ ciphertext letter } = \text{ plaintext letter } + k\quad\operatorname{mod}26 ciphertext letter = plaintext letter +kmod26

    • To decrypt,  plaintext letter = ciphertext letter −kmod⁡26\text{ plaintext letter } = \text{ ciphertext letter } - k\quad\operatorname{mod}26 plaintext letter = ciphertext letter −kmod26

    • The key is k mod⁡26k\:\operatorname{mod}26kmod26.

  • Cryptosystem objectives:

    • Secrecy: the intercepted message should be not able to be decrypted

    • Integrity: a message should not allowed to be altered without the receiver knowing

    • Authenticity: the receiver should be certain of the identity of the sender

    • Non-repudiation: the sender should not be able to claim they sent a message; the receiver should be able to prove they did.

  • Kerckhoff's principle: a cryptographic system should be secure even if the details of the system are known to an attacker.

  • Types of attack:

    • Ciphertext-only: the plaintext is deduced from the ciphertext.

    • Known-plaintext: intercepted ciphertext and associated stolen plaintext are used to determine the key.

    • Chosen-plaintext: an attacker tricks a sender into encrypting various chosen plaintexts and observes the ciphertext, then uses this information to determine the key.

    • Chosen-ciphertext: an attacker tricks the receiver into decrypting various chosen ciphertexts and observes the resulting plaintext, then uses this information to determine the key.

Symmetric key ciphers

  • Converting letters to numbers: treat letters as integers modulo 262626, with A=1A = 1A=1, Z=0≡26 (mod⁡26)Z = 0 \equiv 26\:\left( \operatorname{mod}26 \right)Z=0≡26(mod26). Treat a string of text as a vector of integers modulo 262626.

  • Symmetric key cipher: one in which encryption and decryption keys are equal.

  • Key size: log⁡2(number of possible keys)\log_{2}\left( \text{number of possible keys} \right)log2​(number of possible keys).

  • Substitution cipher: key is permutation of {a,…,z}\left\{ a,\ldots,z \right\}{a,…,z}. Key size is log⁡2(26!)\log_{2}(26!)log2​(26!). It is vulnerable to plaintext attacks and ciphertext-only attacks, since different letters (and letter pairs) occur with different frequencies in English.

  • Stirling's formula: n!≈2πn(ne)nn! \approx \sqrt{2\pi n}\left( \frac{n}{e} \right)^{n}n!≈2πn​(en​)n

  • One-time pad: key is uniformly, independently random sequence of integers mod⁡26\operatorname{mod}26mod26, (k1,k2,…)\left( k_{1},k_{2},\ldots \right)(k1​,k2​,…), it is known to the sender and receiver. If message is (m1,m2,…,mr)\left( m_{1},m_{2},\ldots,m_{r} \right)(m1​,m2​,…,mr​) then ciphertext is (c1,c2,…,cr)=(k1+m1,k2+m2,…,kr+mr)\left( c_{1},c_{2},\ldots,c_{r} \right) = \left( k_{1} + m_{1},k_{2} + m_{2},\ldots,k_{r} + m_{r} \right)(c1​,c2​,…,cr​)=(k1​+m1​,k2​+m2​,…,kr​+mr​). To decrypt the ciphertext, mi=ci−kim_{i} = c_{i} - k_{i}mi​=ci​−ki​. Once (k1,…,kr)\left( k_{1},\ldots,k_{r} \right)(k1​,…,kr​) have been used, they must never be used again.

    • One-time pad is information-theoretically secure against ciphertext-only attack: P(M=m ∣ C=c)=P(M=m){\mathbb{P}}(M = m~|~C = c) = {\mathbb{P}}(M = m)P(M=m ∣ C=c)=P(M=m).

    • Disadvantage is keys must never be reused, so must be as long as message.

    • Keys must be truly random.

  • Chinese remainder theorem: let m,n∈Nm,n \in {\mathbb{N}}m,n∈N coprime, a,b∈Za,b \in {\mathbb{Z}}a,b∈Z. Then exists unique solution x mod⁡mnx{\:\operatorname{mod}mn}xmodmn to the congruences x≡amod⁡mx≡bmod⁡n\begin{aligned} x \equiv a & {\quad\operatorname{mod}m} \\ x \equiv b & {\quad\operatorname{mod}n} \end{aligned}x≡ax≡b​modmmodn​

  • Block cipher: group characters in plaintext into blocks of nnn (the block length) and encrypt each block with a key. So plaintext p=(p1,p2,…)p = \left( p_{1},p_{2},\ldots \right)p=(p1​,p2​,…) is divided into blocks P1,P2,…P_{1},P_{2},\ldotsP1​,P2​,… where P1=(p1,…,pn)P_{1} = \left( p_{1},\ldots,p_{n} \right)P1​=(p1​,…,pn​), P2=(pn+1,…,p2n)P_{2} = \left( p_{n + 1},\ldots,p_{2n} \right)P2​=(pn+1​,…,p2n​). Then ciphertext blocks are given by Ci=f(key,Pi)C_{i} = f\left( \text{key},P_{i} \right)Ci​=f(key,Pi​) for some encryption function fff.

  • Hill cipher:

    • Plaintext divided into blocks P1,…,PrP_{1},\ldots,P_{r}P1​,…,Pr​ of length nnn.

    • Each block represented as vector Pi∈(Z/26Z)nP_{i} \in ({\mathbb{Z}}/26{\mathbb{Z}})^{n}Pi​∈(Z/26Z)n

    • Key is invertible n×nn \times nn×n matrix MMM with elements in Z/26Z{\mathbb{Z}}/26{\mathbb{Z}}Z/26Z.

    • Ciphertext for block PiP_{i}Pi​ is Ci=MPiC_{i} = MP_{i}Ci​=MPi​ It can be decrypted with Pi=M−1CP_{i} = M^{- 1}CPi​=M−1C.

    • Let P=(P1,…,Pr)P = \left( P_{1},\ldots,P_{r} \right)P=(P1​,…,Pr​), C=(C1,…,Cr)C = \left( C_{1},\ldots,C_{r} \right)C=(C1​,…,Cr​), then C=MPC = MPC=MP.

  • Confusion: each character of ciphertext depends on many characters of key.

  • Diffusion: each character of ciphertext depends on many characters of plaintext. Ideal diffusion changes a proportion of (S−1)/S(S - 1)/S(S−1)/S of the characters of the ciphertext, where SSS is the number of possible symbols.

  • For Hill cipher, iiith character of ciphertext depends on iiith row of key - this is medium confusion. If jjjth character of plaintext changes and Mij≠0M_{ij} \neq 0Mij​=0 then iiith character of ciphertext changes. MijM_{ij}Mij​ is non-zero with probability roughly 25/2625/2625/26 so good diffusion.

  • Hill cipher is susceptible to known plaintext attack:

    • If P=(P1,…,Pn)P = \left( P_{1},\ldots,P_{n} \right)P=(P1​,…,Pn​) are nnn blocks of plaintext with length nnn such that PPP is invertible and we know PPP and the corresponding CCC, then we can recover MMM, since C=MP⟹M=CP−1C = MP \Longrightarrow M = CP^{- 1}C=MP⟹M=CP−1.

    • If enough blocks of ciphertext are intercepted, it is very likely that nnn of them will produce an invertible matrix PPP.

Public key cryptography and the RSA algorithm

  • Euler φ\varphiφ function: φ:N→N,φ(n)=∣{1≤a≤n:gcd⁡(a,n)=1}∣=∣(Z/nZ)×∣\varphi:{\mathbb{N}} \rightarrow {\mathbb{N}},\varphi(n) = \left| \left\{ 1 \leq a \leq n:\gcd(a,n) = 1 \right\} \right| = \left| ({\mathbb{Z}}/n{\mathbb{Z}})^{\times} \right|φ:N→N,φ(n)=∣{1≤a≤n:gcd(a,n)=1}∣=∣(Z/nZ)×∣

  • φ(pr)=pr−pr−1\varphi(p^{r}) = p^{r} - p^{r - 1}φ(pr)=pr−pr−1, φ(mn)=φ(m)φ(n)\varphi(mn) = \varphi(m)\varphi(n)φ(mn)=φ(m)φ(n) for gcd⁡(m,n)=1\gcd(m,n) = 1gcd(m,n)=1.

  • Euler's theorem: if gcd⁡(a,n)=1\gcd(a,n) = 1gcd(a,n)=1, aφ(n)≡1(mod⁡n)a^{\varphi(n)} \equiv 1\quad\left( \operatorname{mod}n \right)aφ(n)≡1(modn).

  • Public key cryptography:

    • Create two keys, kDk_{D}kD​ and kEk_{E}kE​. kEk_{E}kE​ is public, kDk_{D}kD​ is private.

    • Plaintext mmm is encrypted as c=f(m,kE)c = f\left( m,k_{E} \right)c=f(m,kE​).

    • Ciphertext decrypted by m=g(c,kD)m = g\left( c,k_{D} \right)m=g(c,kD​).

  • RSA:

    • kEk_{E}kE​ is pair (n,e)(n,e)(n,e) where n=pqn = pqn=pq is product of two distinct primes and e∈Ze \in {\mathbb{Z}}e∈Z is coprime to φ(n)\varphi(n)φ(n).

    • kDk_{D}kD​ is integer ddd such that de≡1(mod⁡φ(n))de \equiv 1\quad\left( \operatorname{mod}\varphi(n) \right)de≡1(modφ(n)).

    • mmm is an integer modulo nnn, mmm and nnn are coprime.

    • Encryption: c=me(mod⁡n)c = m^{e}\quad\left( \operatorname{mod}n \right)c=me(modn).

    • Decryption: m=cd(mod⁡n)m = c^{d}\quad\left( \operatorname{mod}n \right)m=cd(modn).

  • RSA problem: given n=pqn = pqn=pq a product of two unknown primes, eee and me(mod⁡n)m^{e}\quad\left( \operatorname{mod}n \right)me(modn), recover mmm. If nnn can be factored, the RSA is solved.

  • It is recommended that nnn have at least 204820482048 bits. A typical choice of eee is 216+12^{16} + 1216+1.

  • Attacks on RSA:

    • If you can factor nnn, you can compute ddd, so can break RSA (as then you know φ(n)\varphi(n)φ(n) so can compute e−1(mod⁡φ(n))e^{- 1}\quad\left( \operatorname{mod}\varphi(n) \right)e−1(modφ(n))).

    • If φ(n)\varphi(n)φ(n) is known, then we have pq=npq = npq=n and (p−1)(q−1)=φ(n)(p - 1)(q - 1) = \varphi(n)(p−1)(q−1)=φ(n) so p+q=n−φ(n)+1p + q = n - \varphi(n) + 1p+q=n−φ(n)+1. Hence ppp and qqq are roots of x2−(n−φ(n)+1)+n=0x^{2} - \left( n - \varphi(n) + 1 \right) + n = 0x2−(n−φ(n)+1)+n=0.

    • Known ddd: we have de−1de - 1de−1 is multiple of φ(n)\varphi(n)φ(n). Look for a factor AAA of de−1de - 1de−1 such that (p−1) ∣ A(p - 1)~|~A(p−1) ∣ A, (q−1)∤A(q - 1) \nmid A(q−1)∤A. Then try xA−1x^{A} - 1xA−1 for random xxx, this satisfies xA−1x^{A} - 1xA−1 is divisible by ppp, hence gcd⁡(xA−1,n)=p\gcd(x^{A} - 1,n) = pgcd(xA−1,n)=p.

  • RSA signatures:

    • Public key is (n,e)(n,e)(n,e) and private key is ddd.

    • When sending a message mmm, message is signed by also sending s=mdmod⁡ns = m^{d}\operatorname{mod}ns=mdmodn.

    • (m,s)(m,s)(m,s) is received, verified by checking if m=semod⁡nm = s^{e}\operatorname{mod}nm=semodn.

    • Forging a signature on a message mmm would require finding sss with m=semod⁡nm = s^{e}\operatorname{mod}nm=semodn. This is the RSA problem.

    • However, choosing signature sss first then taking m=semod⁡nm = s^{e}\operatorname{mod}nm=semodn.

    • To solve this, (m,s)(m,s)(m,s) is sent where s=h(m)^ds = h(m)\hat{}ds=h(m)^d, hhh is hash function. Then the message receiver verifies h(m)=semod⁡nh(m) = s^{e}\operatorname{mod}nh(m)=semodn.

    • Now, for a signature to be forged, an attacker would have to find mmm with h(m)=semod⁡nh(m) = s^{e}\operatorname{mod}nh(m)=semodn.

  • Hash function is function h:{messages}→Hh:\left\{ \text{messages} \right\} \rightarrow \mathcal{H}h:{messages}→H that:

    • Can be computed efficiently

    • Is preimage-resistant: can't quickly find mmm with given h(m)h(m)h(m).

    • Is collision-resistant: can't quickly find m,m′m,m\primem,m′ with h(m)=h(m′)h(m) = h(m\prime)h(m)=h(m′).

    Example is SHA-256.

  • Theorem: it is no easier to find φ(n)\varphi(n)φ(n) than to factorise nnn.

  • Theorem: it is no easier to find ddd than to factor nnn.

  • Miller-Rabin algorithm:

    1. Choose random xmod⁡nx\operatorname{mod}nxmodn.

    2. Let n−1=2rsn - 1 = 2^{r}sn−1=2rs, y=xsy = x^{s}y=xs.

    3. Compute y,y2,…,y2rmod⁡ny,y^{2},\ldots,y^{2^{r}}\operatorname{mod}ny,y2,…,y2rmodn.

    4. If 111 isn't in this list, nnn is composite (with witness xxx).

    5. If 111 is in list preceded bynumber other than ±1\pm 1±1, nnn is composite (with witness aaa).

    6. Other, nnn is possible prime (to base xxx).

Factorisation

  • Trial division algorithm: for p=2,3,5,…p = 2,3,5,\ldotsp=2,3,5,… test whether p ∣ np~|~np ∣ n.

  • Fermat's method:

    • Let a=⌈n⌉a = \left\lceil \sqrt{n} \right\rceila=⌈n​⌉. Compute a2mod⁡na^{2}\operatorname{mod}na2modn, (a+1)2mod⁡n(a + 1)^{2}\operatorname{mod}n(a+1)2modn until a square x2≡(a+i)2mod⁡nx^{2} \equiv (a + i)^{2}\operatorname{mod}nx2≡(a+i)2modn appears. Then compute gcd⁡(a+i−x,n)\gcd(a + i - x,n)gcd(a+i−x,n).

    • Works well under special conditions on the factors: if ∣p−q∣≤22n4|p - q| \leq 2\sqrt{2}\sqrt[4]{n}∣p−q∣≤22​4n​ then Fermat's method takes one step: x=⌈n⌉x = \left\lceil \sqrt{n} \right\rceilx=⌈n​⌉ works.

  • An integer is BBB-smooth if all its prime factors are ≤B\leq B≤B.

  • Quadratic sieve:

    • Choose BBB and let mmm be numberof primes ≤B\leq B≤B.

    • Look at integers x=⌈n⌉+kx = \left\lceil \sqrt{n} \right\rceil + kx=⌈n​⌉+k, k=1,2,…k = 1,2,\ldotsk=1,2,… and check whether y=x2−ny = x^{2} - ny=x2−n is BBB-smooth.

    • Once y1=x12−n,…,yt=xt2−ny_{1} = x_{1}^{2} - n,\ldots,y_{t} = x_{t}^{2} - ny1​=x12​−n,…,yt​=xt2​−n are all BBB-smooth with t>mt > mt>m, find some product of them that is a square.

    • Deduce a congruence between the squares.

  • Other factorisation algorithms:

    • Pollard's ρ\rhoρ algorithm.

    • Pollard's p−1p - 1p−1 algorithm.

    • Lenstra's algorithm using elliptic curves.

    • General number field sieve

    • Shor's algorithm: ln⁡(N)2ln⁡(ln⁡(N)){\ln(N)}^{2}\ln(\ln(N))ln(N)2ln(ln(N)).

Primitive roots

  • Let ppp prime, g∈Fp×g \in {\mathbb{F}}_{p}^{\times}g∈Fp×​. Order of ggg is smallest a∈N0a \in {\mathbb{N}}_{0}a∈N0​ such that ga=1g^{a} = 1ga=1. ggg is primitive root if its order is p−1p - 1p−1.

  • Let ppp prime, g∈Fp×g \in {\mathbb{F}}_{p}^{\times}g∈Fp×​ primitive root. If x∈Fp×x \in {\mathbb{F}}_{p}^{\times}x∈Fp×​ then x=gLx = g^{L}x=gL for some 0≤L<p−10 \leq L < p - 10≤L<p−1. Then LLL is discrete logarithm of xxx to base ggg. Write L=Lg(x)L = L_{g}(x)L=Lg​(x). It satisfies:

    • gLg(x)≡x(mod⁡p)g^{L_{g}(x)} \equiv x\quad\left( \operatorname{mod}p \right)gLg​(x)≡x(modp) and ga≡x(mod⁡p)⟺a≡Lg(x)(mod⁡p−1)g^{a} \equiv x\quad\left( \operatorname{mod}p \right) \Longleftrightarrow a \equiv L_{g}(x)\quad\left( \operatorname{mod}p - 1 \right)ga≡x(modp)⟺a≡Lg​(x)(modp−1).

    • Lg(1)=0L_{g}(1) = 0Lg​(1)=0, Lg(g)=1L_{g}(g) = 1Lg​(g)=1.

    • Lg(xy)≡Lg(x)+Lg(y)(mod⁡p−1)L_{g}(xy) \equiv L_{g}(x) + L_{g}(y)\quad\left( \operatorname{mod}p - 1 \right)Lg​(xy)≡Lg​(x)+Lg​(y)(modp−1).

    • hhh is primitive root mod ppp iff Lg(h)L_{g}(h)Lg​(h) coprime to p−1p - 1p−1. So number of primitive roots mod ppp is φ(p−1)\varphi(p - 1)φ(p−1).

  • Discrete logarithm problem: given p,g,xp,g,xp,g,x, compute Lg(x)L_{g}(x)Lg​(x).

  • Diffie-Hellman key exchange:

    • Two parties agree on prime ppp and primitive root gmod⁡pg\operatorname{mod}pgmodp.

    • Alice chooses secret αmod⁡(p−1)\alpha\operatorname{mod}(p - 1)αmod(p−1) and sends gαmod⁡pg^{\alpha}\operatorname{mod}pgαmodp to Bob.

    • Bob chooses secret βmod⁡(p−1)\beta\operatorname{mod}(p - 1)βmod(p−1) and sends gβmod⁡pg^{\beta}\operatorname{mod}pgβmodp to Alice.

    • Alice and Bob both compute κ=gαβ=(gα)β)=(gβ)αmod⁡p\kappa = g^{\alpha\beta} = \left( g^{\alpha} \right)^{\beta}) = \left( g^{\beta} \right)^{\alpha}\operatorname{mod}pκ=gαβ=(gα)β)=(gβ)αmodp.

  • Diffie-Hellman problem: given p,g,gα,gβp,g,g^{\alpha},g^{\beta}p,g,gα,gβ, compute gαβg^{\alpha\beta}gαβ.

  • If discrete logarithm problem cna be solved, so can Diffie-Hellman problem (since could compute α=Lg(ga)\alpha = L_{g}\left( g^{a} \right)α=Lg​(ga) or β=Lg(gβ)\beta = L_{g}\left( g^{\beta} \right)β=Lg​(gβ)).

  • Elgamal public key encryption:

    • Alice chooses prime ppp, primitive root ggg, private key α  mod⁡(p−1)\alpha\:\:\operatorname{mod}(p - 1)αmod(p−1).

    • Her public key is y=gαy = g^{\alpha}y=gα.

    • Bob chooses random kmod⁡(p−1)k\operatorname{mod}(p - 1)kmod(p−1)

    • To send message mmm (integer mod ppp), he sends the pair (r,m′)=(gk,myk)(r,m\prime) = \left( g^{k},my^{k} \right)(r,m′)=(gk,myk).

    • To descript the message, Alice computes rα=gαk=ykr^{\alpha} = g^{\alpha k} = y^{k}rα=gαk=yk and then m=m′y−k=m′r−αm = m\prime y^{- k} = m\prime r^{- \alpha}m=m′y−k=m′r−α.

    • If Diffie-Hellman problem is hard, then Elgamal encryption is secure against known plaintext attack.

    • Key kkk must be random and different each time.

  • Decision Diffie-Hellman problem: given ga,gb,cg^{a},g^{b},cga,gb,c in Fp×{\mathbb{F}}_{p}^{\times}Fp×​, decide whether c=gabc = g^{ab}c=gab.

    • This problem is not always hard, as can tell if gabg^{ab}gab is square or not. Can fix this by taking ggg to have large prime order q ∣ (p−1)q~|~(p - 1)q ∣ (p−1). p=2q+1p = 2q + 1p=2q+1 is a good choice.

  • Elgamal signatures:

    • Public key is (p,g)(p,g)(p,g), y=gαy = g^{\alpha}y=gα for private key α\alphaα.

    • Valid Elgamal signature on mmm is pair (r,s)(r,s)(r,s), r≥0r \geq 0r≥0, s<p−1s < p - 1s<p−1 such that yrrs=gm(mod⁡p)y^{r}r^{s} = g^{m}\quad\left( \operatorname{mod}p \right)yrrs=gm(modp)

    • Alice computes r=gkr = g^{k}r=gk, k∈(Z/(p−1))×k \in \left( {\mathbb{Z}}/(p - 1) \right)^{\times}k∈(Z/(p−1))× random.

    • Then gαrgks≡gmmod⁡pg^{\alpha r}g^{ks} \equiv g^{m}\quad\operatorname{mod}pgαrgks≡gmmodp so αr+ks≡m(mod⁡p−1)\alpha r + ks \equiv m\quad\left( \operatorname{mod}p - 1 \right)αr+ks≡m(modp−1) so s=k−1(m−αr)mod⁡p−1s = k^{- 1}(m - \alpha r)\quad\operatorname{mod}p - 1s=k−1(m−αr)modp−1.

  • Elgamal signature problem: given p,g,y,mp,g,y,mp,g,y,m, find r,sr,sr,s such that yrrs=my^{r}r^{s} = myrrs=m.

  • Discrete logarithm problem: given prime ppp, primitive root g mod⁡pg\:\operatorname{mod}pgmodp, x∈Fp×x \in {\mathbb{F}}_{p}^{\times}x∈Fp×​, calculate Lg(x)L_{g}(x)Lg​(x).

  • Baby-step giant-step algorithm for solving DLP:

    • Let N=⌈p−1⌉N = \left\lceil \sqrt{p - 1} \right\rceilN=⌈p−1​⌉.

    • Baby-steps: compute gj mod⁡pg^{j}\:\operatorname{mod}pgjmodp for 0≤j<N0 \leq j < N0≤j<N.

    • Giant-steps: compute xg−Nk mod⁡pxg^{- Nk}\:\operatorname{mod}pxg−Nkmodp for 0≤k<N0 \leq k < N0≤k<N.

    • Look for a match between baby-steps and giant-steps lists: gj=xg−Nk⟹x=gj+Nkg^{j} = xg^{- Nk} \Longrightarrow x = g^{j + Nk}gj=xg−Nk⟹x=gj+Nk.

    • Always works since if x=gLx = g^{L}x=gL for 0≤L<p−1≤N20 \leq L < p - 1 \leq N^{2}0≤L<p−1≤N2, so LLL can be written as j+Nkj + Nkj+Nk with j,k∈{0,…,N−1}j,k \in \left\{ 0,\ldots,N - 1 \right\}j,k∈{0,…,N−1}.
